chain_of_thought: Stack shows crash in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, called from ASN.1→X509 parsing chain within libUbikeyJni.so; JNI bridge jGetKeyUsage is the public entry, fuzz harness just replaces app Java.

Tooling failed to decompile functions (404 errors), so I cannot see exact code or memcpy calls; however, symbolic names are descriptive: ASN.1 child-number computation in certificate loader suggests logic around traversing ASN.1 trees.

Such functions usually walk length/tag fields of attacker-controlled ASN.1 blobs (certificates or keys). Fuzzer likely feeds arbitrary ASN.1, mimicking untrusted certificates in real app usage (e.g., smart card, network).

Crash type is only “Process Termination: Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum”; no explicit OOB/SEGV/sanitizer message, so this might be an internal bounds-check failure intentionally aborting if an index is out of range, i.e., a safe but fatal error path rather than memory corruption.

Without decompilation I cannot confirm that the termination arises from a memory-unsafe operation (like uninformed array index) as opposed to an explicit abort() after validating child index/length. Name “Compute_*_ChildNum” also reads like pure calculation; it may throw/abort when encountering inconsistent structure.

JNI bridge jGetKeyUsage presumably accepts some form of certificate/ASN.1 from Java; fuzz harness probably bypasses any real-world validation and may provide impossible/invalid buffer sizes or nulls, influencing behaviour.

Given lack of code and exact crash reason, evidence that this is exploitable memory corruption is weak. It may be a controlled assertion/abort on malformed input (benign DoS under local context) rather than a vulnerability under realistic, non-fuzzing conditions.
Therefore classify as not a confirmed vulnerability, with low confidence of actual memory safety issue; mark as non-vulnerable pending deeper binary analysis with real decompilation or crash logs.
reasons: Crash occurs in internal ASN.1 child-number computation during certificate loading, which may intentionally abort on malformed structures.
No visibility into unsafe memory operations or out-of-bounds access due to decompilation failure; termination reason does not clearly indicate memory corruption.
Fuzz harness may be feeding unreachable or structurally impossible inputs compared to real app usage, leading to internal consistency check failure rather than exploitable bug.
recommendations: Re-run analysis with full native symbols and decompilation to inspect Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum for unchecked indexing or pointer arithmetic.
Collect precise crash type (signal, fault address, registers) to distinguish assertion/abort from genuine memory access violation.
Add robust input validation and structured error handling around certificate parsing in CERT_Load and upstream Java before JNI calls.
assumptions: Fuzzer simulates arbitrary ASN.1 certificate blobs into jGetKeyUsage.
Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum likely processes ASN.1 structure metadata and may include internal sanity checks.
Real-world Java callers may constrain or validate inputs before reaching JNI, unlike fuzz harness.
limitations: Could not decompile native functions due to tool server 404 errors, limiting code-level inspection.
No concrete crash signal, register state, or memory map to prove out-of-bounds memory access.
No Java call-graph details beyond the single JNI bridge name, so real input sources and validation are unknown.

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in __memcpy with preceding frames all in app libUbikeyJni.so: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> Get_DER_Child -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage. That strongly suggests an out-of-bounds memory access in ASN.1/DER certificate parsing. Even though MCP search errored, function names and order are clear from Library Map.

CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT almost certainly take attacker-controlled certificate/DER bytes coming from Java via JNI (UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage is likely given a blob or file with certificate data). Fuzzer harness fuzz_one_input mimics that untrusted input. Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and Get_DER_Child are typical parsing helpers; the memcpy crash indicates they copy fields based on lengths parsed from DER without sufficient bounds checking against the backing buffer or destination structure sizes.

Because memcpy is crashing, the bug is likely an out-of-bounds read or write caused by a length or pointer derived from the DER data. DER is attacker-controlled in typical smart card/Ubikey scenarios (remote server, NFC token, or file). There is no indication that Java-side performs strict DER validation or length capping before calling JNI, and the call chain is short (JNI -> CERT_Load -> parsing helpers). That makes this a realistic memory corruption vulnerability rather than a harness-only issue.

No evidence that this is a deliberate abort or sanitizer; it is a low-level memcpy fault. Stack shows no malloc/sanitizer frames, just memcpy and parsing code. Fuzzer entry fuzz_one_input simply replaces app logic that would provide the certificate data. Thus the same parsing path will be reachable in production when the app processes certificates or key usage extensions. Impact is at least app-level DoS and plausibly exploitable heap/stack corruption (depending on where memcpy writes), so high severity. Exact exploit proof (e.g., RCE) cannot be given without disassembly, but the pattern maps strongly to CWE-787/125 style bugs.

Given missing MCP details (404 errors), I cannot quote exact C code or argument expressions, but the structural evidence (untrusted DER -> certificate parser helpers -> memcpy crash) is sufficient to classify as a likely memory safety vulnerability. Confidence slightly below 1.0 due to lack of direct code view but still high (0.9).
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from DER/ASN.1 certificate parsing helpers in app lib
DER/ASN.1 lengths and offsets are derived from attacker-controlled certificate data passed through JNI
No evidence of bounds checking before memcpy; crash implies out-of-bounds read/write
Fuzzer harness models realistic untrusted inputs (certificate blobs) rather than impossible states
Likely leads to memory corruption and at least app-level denial of service
recommendations: Audit Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child, and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT for unchecked memcpy/memmove calls and missing length validation
Ensure all length fields parsed from DER are validated against the buffer size and destination structure sizes before copying
Add robust ASN.1/DER parsing with bounds checks or use a well-tested library instead of custom memcpy-based parsing
assumptions: UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage ultimately processes untrusted certificate or key usage data from user/network/smart card
No comprehensive size validation is done in Java before invoking the JNI function
The crash corresponds to an out-of-bounds access in memcpy rather than unrelated hardware faults
limitations: Could not decompile the exact native functions due to MCP 404 errors
No direct view of Java UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage signature or argument preparation
Exact memory layout (heap vs stack, overwrite direction) of the memcpy destination is unknown

exploit.trigger_method: Malicious or malformed DER-encoded certificate/key-usage blob passed to UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage via app workflow
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to load or process a chosen DER/ASN.1 certificate or key-usage structure
Target device runs a vulnerable version of com.skt.smartbill with libUbikeyJni.so
No additional sandboxing prevents exploitation of native memory corruption
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER-encoded certificate or key-usage extension with manipulated length/offset fields that cause an overlong memcpy in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or Get_DER_Child
Deliver this crafted certificate to the app via the normal input channel (e.g., file import, network response, or smart card/U2F interaction) so that UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage is invoked on it
When CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT parse the DER, the malformed lengths trigger out-of-bounds memcpy, corrupting adjacent memory
Refine the DER payload to control overwritten data (e.g., heap metadata or function pointers) to move from crash to controlled code execution
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.skt.smartbill/.MainActivity --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/crafted_cert.der
exploit.notes: Exact activity name and intent extra key are placeholders; actual entrypoint and parameter names must be obtained from the app's Java code or manifest.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy, next is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST inside libUbikeyJni.so, so first app-level frame is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.

I attempted to locate Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, CERT_Load, and the JNI symbol in Ghidra via search_functions_by_name but the endpoint returned 404 for those queries, so I cannot see decompiled code or exact memcpy arguments.

Given only the stack, I infer the crash is a memcpy-related fault while decoding a DER ASN.1 structure in CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, likely due to trusting length fields from attacker-controlled DER data without full bounds checks.

JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage suggests this is reading the KeyUsage extension from a certificate, which is normally based on untrusted certificate data (e.g., server or external token), so the DER parser is operating on attacker-influenced input in realistic app flows.

Fuzz harness fuzz_one_input replaced the usual higher-level caller but JNI entry and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST are actual library code, not harness-only; memcpy in a DER decoder that crashes indicates probable out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer due to missing validation of length/offset fields.

Without code I cannot prove whether the memcpy length is fully controlled versus partially derived, but experience with similar DER parsing issues and the crash at memcpy (not an explicit abort) suggests real memory safety risk rather than a deliberate bounds check failure.

Because this occurs in certificate loading (CERT_Load) and could be triggered by a malicious or corrupted certificate presented to the app, it is a potentially remotely-triggerable memory corruption; severity is therefore high even if exact exploitability (RCE vs DoS) is unclear.
Due to missing decompiler views, I cannot supply exact expressions for src/dst/len or concrete Java call graph, so confidence is moderate (0.7) rather than near-certain.
There is no evidence that the crash is purely a harness artifact like passing null where app never would; instead, fuzzing just accelerated reaching a realistic bug path in DER decoding.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST while decoding DER certificate data, indicating a memory copy with invalid bounds or pointer.
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load process certificate/DER input that is plausibly attacker-controlled (malicious or malformed certificates).
JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage is a real app entry into this code, so the fault is not specific to the fuzz harness but in production logic.
No evidence of deliberate abort or harness-only misuse; memcpy crash strongly suggests out-of-bounds read/write (CWE-787/788) or similar memory safety error.
recommendations: Reverse-engineer Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load to identify all memcpy/memmove calls that use DER-derived lengths and add strict bounds checks against the underlying buffer allocation size.
Introduce centralized DER parsing with robust validation (rejecting overlong, negative, or nested length fields) before any memcpy into fixed-size structures.
Add fuzz tests for certificate/DER inputs around Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage to ensure malformed inputs cannot trigger memory corruption.
assumptions: Certificate/DER data processed by CERT_Load originates from untrusted or semi-untrusted sources (e.g., remote servers, external tokens, files).
The memcpy crash is due to out-of-bounds or invalid pointer rather than a deliberate abort within a hardened allocator or sanitizer.
The fuzz harness passes data in a way consistent with some real-world paths (lengths not forcibly zero or guaranteed-safe by the harness).
limitations: Could not decompile Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, CERT_Load, or the JNI method due to tool endpoint errors, so memcpy argument derivation is inferred from naming and stack only.
No Java-side call graph or argument construction was available, so exact attacker control over buffer length/contents is partially speculative.
Missing concrete register/memory state at crash, preventing precise classification between OOB-read vs OOB-write or null-deref.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a malicious or malformed DER-encoded certificate or key material to the app path invoking jGetKeyUsage
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to parse an attacker-chosen certificate or ASN.1-encoded blob via the UbikeyJni jGetKeyUsage path.
The vulnerable memcpy in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST uses lengths at least partially derived from DER length fields without complete bounds checks.
The environment is non-hardened enough that out-of-bounds writes/reads can influence control flow or stability.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER-encoded certificate or ASN.1 blob with malformed/oversized length fields targeting the structure parsed by Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.
Deliver this blob to the app via any feature that results in Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage being called on it (e.g., importing a certificate, reading a smartcard response).
When the app invokes jGetKeyUsage, CERT_Load passes the malicious DER buffer to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, which performs an unchecked memcpy based on attacker-influenced lengths.
The unchecked memcpy causes memory corruption or access violation, leading to process crash (DoS) and potentially controllable memory overwrite if heap layout is favorable.
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.skt.smartbill/.<MainOrImportActivity> --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/malicious_cert.der
exploit.notes: Activity name and exact mechanism to load a certificate are unknown; replace <MainOrImportActivity> with the component that triggers jGetKeyUsage. Initial PoC would likely demonstrate crash/DoS rather than code execution; further heap grooming and analysis would be required for RCE.
